coredata.dc:title: Photoluminescence carbon nano dots for the conductivity based optical sensing of dopamine and bioimaging applications
coredata.dc:creator: Jayasekhar Babu, Punuri
Saranya, Sibyala
Singh, Yengkhom Disco
Venkataswamy, Mallepogu
Raichur, Ashok M.
Doble, Mukesh
coredata.dc:description: We demonstrate the use of custard apple peel waste biomass (CAPWB) to synthesize the homogenous carbon nano dots (CNDs) for the conductivity-based dopamine sensing and bioimaging applications. In this study, CNDs are synthesized hydrothermally using microwave irradiation and they are found to be biocompatible and hemocompatible. The formation of CNDs was confirmed by UV–Visible spectroscopy analysis. TEM images indicates that they are spherical in shape and size ranging from 3 to 8 nm. XRD and FT-IR studies confirmed the poor crystalline nature and different functional groups of these CNDs, respectively. Fluorescence spectroscopy studies proved their ability to exhibit wavelength dependent photoluminescence (PL) property and showed the PL phenomenon in C2C12 (mouse myoblast), 3T3 (swiss mouse), HeLa (cervical cancer) cells and BL-21 (E. coli strain), suggesting that these CNDs can be used for live cell bioimaging. Cytotoxicity and hemo compatibility assays reveal that these CNDs are not toxic to the cells, providing an opportunity to use them in biomedical applications. Importantly, the CND-Fe complex exhibited dopamine (DA) dependent conductivity-based sensing, thus, providing the novel route for the detection of DA.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Dopamine
Optical materials
Nanosensors
Carbon nano dots
Bioimaging
Optical sensors
Hemo compatibility

Effect of pH on the CNDs PL property
Effect of synthesized CNDs on RBC morphology
Effect of synthesized CNDs on RBC lysis
Sensing of dopamine based on conductivity
Sensing of dopamine based on conductivity

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Dr. Punuri J Babu would like to acknowledge Science and Engineering Research Board (SERB) for providing the financial support vide project no: SRG/2020/002283 and also Central Instrumentation Facility (CIF) , IIT Madras .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Absorption (UV–Vis) spectra of carbon nano dots synthesized using custard apple peel waste biomass.
(a) TEM (Scale bar 10 nm) (b) DLS particle size distribution (c) HR-TEM (d) SAED pattern of CNDs.
(a) XRD and (b) FT-IR pattern of CNDs synthesized using custard apple peel waste biomass.
Wavelength dependent PL behavior of CNDs (a) PL increased from 230 to 290 nm (b) PL decreased from 300 to 350 nm.
PL emission of CNDs synthesized using custard apple peel waste biomass at different pH.
CNDs exhibits PL in different mammalian cells and bacterial cells (Scale bar 20 μm).
(a) Morphology of the RBC incubated with different concentrations of CNDs at different time intervals (Scale Bar 20 μm).(b) Hemolysis of RBC at two different concentrations of CNDs.
Cytotoxicity assay: Viability of L929 and HeLa cells exposed for 24 h at different concentrations of CNDs (10–100 μg/ml).
(a) PL of the CNDs with Fe(II) and DA (b) Conductivity of the CNDs in the presence of iron ions (Fe2+) at various DA concentrations.
Utilization of custard apple peel waste biomass (CAPWB) for dopamine sensing and live cell imaging.
The list of various methods demonstrating the detection of dopamine.

MoS2 QDs as fluorophore and MoS2 nanosheets as quencher
Fluorescent label‐free detection with Ru complex and QDs
Fluorescent sensor with CDs and nano‐graphite

head.ce:title: Photoluminescence carbon nano dots for the conductivity based optical sensing of dopamine and bioimaging applications

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a

Bhupat and Jyoti Mehta School of Biosciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Bio Engineering and Drug Design Laboratory, Department of Biotechnology, Bhupat and Jyoti Mehta School of Biosciences, Indian Institute of Technology Madras, Chennai, 600 036, Tamil Nadu, India

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Biomaterials and Bioengineering Research Laboratory, Department of Biotechnology, Mizoram University, Pachhunga University College Campus, Aizawl-796 001, Mizoram, India.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We demonstrate the use of custard apple peel waste biomass (CAPWB) to synthesize the homogenous carbon nano dots (CNDs) for the conductivity-based dopamine sensing and bioimaging applications. In this study, CNDs are synthesized hydrothermally using microwave irradiation and they are found to be biocompatible and hemocompatible. The formation of CNDs was confirmed by UV–Visible spectroscopy analysis. TEM images indicates that they are spherical in shape and size ranging from 3 to 8 nm. XRD and FT-IR studies confirmed the poor crystalline nature and different functional groups of these CNDs, respectively. Fluorescence spectroscopy studies proved their ability to exhibit wavelength dependent photoluminescence (PL) property and showed the PL phenomenon in C2C12 (mouse myoblast), 3T3 (swiss mouse), HeLa (cervical cancer) cells and BL-21 (E. coli strain), suggesting that these CNDs can be used for live cell bioimaging. Cytotoxicity and hemo compatibility assays reveal that these CNDs are not toxic to the cells, providing an opportunity to use them in biomedical applications. Importantly, the CND-Fe complex exhibited dopamine (DA) dependent conductivity-based sensing, thus, providing the novel route for the detection of DA.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, biomass is gaining an importance as it is used as a source of biogas and biofuel production [1,2]. Even though there has been an adept awareness in using biomass, however, its use is very limited to the production of renewable energy. According to Food and Agriculture Organization (FAO), each year near about 1.3 billion tons of foods (biomass) are wasted worldwide [3,4]. In addition to that household biomass is getting waste; especially biomass leftover after the consumption of fruits, is not being used for any purpose. Custard apple fruit peel waste is a fine example for such waste which gives huge biomass after its pulp has been used in fruit processing industries and even personal consumption. Based on the fact that the biomass is a huge source of carbon; we have hypothesized the use of waste custard apple peel biomass as a carbon source for the generation of carbon nano materials, especially carbon nano dots (CNDs) in contrast to its use in the production of renewable energy.

Carbon materials are unique materials exhibits allotropy (different molecular structure from the same carbon atoms). The classical example of the allotropic nature of carbon is diamond (hard) and graphite (soft) [5]. Carbon nano dots (CNDs) are the class of carbon nanomaterials having size <10 nm and have gained the interest of researchers as they have numerous applications includes photocatalyst [6], drug carriers [7–9], photothermal therapy [10], biological imaging and as a sensor materials [11–16]. CNDs are being used in wide range of applications due to their tunable fluorescence emission and excitation, excellent biocompatibility, high sensitivity, high quantum yield, high photostability and outstanding water solubility [17]. They can be synthesized using arc discharge [18], Laser Ablation [19], Acidic Oxidation [20], Combustion/Thermal Routes [21], Microwave Pyrolysis [22], Hydrothermal/Solvothermal Synthesis [23,24], Electrochemistry Method [25]. However, these methods are complex, costly, and toxic [26]. On other hand, there are some reports stating about the preparation of CNDs using natural materials [27–34]. However, there is a growing need to explore a new carbon source and cost-effective method for the synthesis of CNDs.

In this study, we have used custard apple peel waste (Annona reticulate, family: Annonaceae) biomass (CAPWB) for the synthesis of CNDs by employing microwave mediated hydrothermal carbonization method. The synthesized CNDs using CAPWB have many advantages and a wide range of applications over CNDs derived from the other sources. The synthesized CNDs are isotropic in nature and exhibited the long-term stability (2 months) under room temperature. On other hand, the synthesized CNDs exhibited a wavelength-dependence behavior and showed increased photoluminescence at different excitation values ranging from 230 to 290 nm. This phenomenon enables them to use for live cells optical imaging in wide range of cell types namely 3T3 Swiss fibro blast, C2C12, HeLa and E. coli. Moreover, hemocompatibility studies suggested that the synthesized CNDs below a concentration of 250 μg/ml are not influencing the morphology or integrity of the RBCs, thus can be used for blood contacting biomedical applications. The cytocompatibility studies revealed that CNDs are nontoxic to the cells tested and showed good compatibility especially when compared to other nano dots for which cytotoxicity has been cited in the literature as a significant concern. This provides the evidence that the synthesized CNDs can be considered as nontoxic for typical cellular experiments. These unique properties enable them to use in biomedical applications. Importantly, these CNDs showed the wavelength and dopamine (DA) concentration dependant conductivity, suggesting the novel route for the conductivity-based sensing/detection of DA. This study explores low cost, facile, eco-friendly and cost-effective method of conversion of neglected or very low value CAPWB into high value and useful materials (CNDs).

All the reagents are obtained from Merck (Mumbai, India) and Himedia (Mumbai, India). All plasticware related cell culture were procured from Sigma (Chennai, India). The different kinds of cell lines namely HeLa, L929, T3T, C2C12 and E. coli (BL-21, microbial strain) were obtained from the NCCS, Pune, India. All the reagents used in this work are analytical and molecular grade. Custard apple (Annona reticulate) was purchased from the velachery market, Chennai, India. 20 mM of HEPES (Sigma, Chennai, India) buffer was prepared and adjusted to a pH 7.

The procedure involves grinding of 500 g of clean custard apple peel waste biomass (CAPWB) by mixing with 1L of distilled water. The resultant mixture was filtered to get the homogenous solution. The homogenous solution was heated using domestic microwave (MW) oven operated at 1200 W, 2.45 GHz (180 Sec ON and 30 Sec OFF mode) for 30 min. The resulted solution was centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 3 min to remove debris. The supernatant containing CNDs was collected and freeze dried in a lyophilizer for 14h to obtain a powder. 1 mg/ml of CNDs were used for the subsequent experiment.

UV–Visible analysis of CNDs: The synthesized CNDs absorption measurements are carried out by the plate reader (Model: 2300–0000, MA, USA). Size and shape analyses of CNDs: CNDs were coated on to the Copper gird and TEM (Tecnai F30 FEI-Eindhoven, Netherlands) was employed to analyze the shape and size. Dynamic light scattering (DLS) analysis: The CNDs were diluted 10 folds using PBS (pH 7.4) and employed DLS (Malvern) for size distribution taking measurement between 0.1 and 10,000 nm. XRD instrument analysis: A portion of microscope glass (1.5 × 1.5 cm) slide was drop casted with CNDs solution and dried at 50 °C using hot air oven. This process was repeated for several times until it formed a thin layer. The crystalline nature of the CNDs was analyzed by the XRD instrument (Bruker, USA). FT-IR analysis: 1 mg of CNDs was ground with appropriate (~100 mg) amount of KBr and perform the FTIR spectroscopy (PerkinElmer, USA) to get the details of various functional groups (resolution of 2 cm−1). PL of CNDs at different wavelength: The PL behavior of the CNDs is observed at various wavelengths using plate reader (EnSpire, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence spectroscopy and microscopy studies of CNDs
ce:section.ce:para: Different mammalian cells (HeLa, C2C12 and L929) were cultured with DMEM medium using CO2 incubator (Heal Force, China) at 37 °C. 0.1 ml of 0.2 μm filtered CNDs (1 mg/1 ml) were added to the cells after 24h of culture and incubated for 4h. The cells were photoed using a fluorescent microscope equipped with interphase camera (Olympus, Model: BX51, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of pH on the CNDs PL property
ce:section.ce:para: 1 ml (1 mg/ml) of CNDs solution is taken in six different dark reagent bottles (20 ml flat bottom) and made up the final volume to 10 ml with HEPES buffer. The pH of the reaction mixtures are adjusted to 1, 3, 5, 7, 9 and 11 using 1 N of HCL and 1 M of NaOH solutions.
Experiments with RBC were performed after the proper institute ethical clearance. Blood was obtained from the voluntary health services (VHS), Taramani, and Chennai, India.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of synthesized CNDs on RBC morphology
ce:section.ce:para: 1 ml of fresh blood is taken in five centrifuge tubes and centrifuged for 5 min at 1000 rpm. The RBC pellet was washed with PBS and then incubated with various concentrations of CNDs (250, 500 and 750 μg/ml). RBC pellet washed with 1 ml of PBS is considered as control. The total volume made up to 1 ml and incubated for 10 min at room temperature. The RBCs are fixed with 4% formaldehyde for 2 h on glass slide and were observed under a microscope. The RBC pellet incubated with water is considered as positive control.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of synthesized CNDs on RBC lysis
ce:section.ce:para:
7 ml of RBC suspension is prepared (16% blood in PBS v/v). 200 μL of RBC suspension was added to three flasks containing 700 μL of PBS. 100 μL of different concentrations of CNDs (250, 500 and 750 μg/ml) were added to each flask. The reaction mixtures are incubated for 45 min in room temperature. The resulted solution was centrifuged for 5 min at 1000 rpm and supernatants (200 μL) of each solution were measured at 540 nm using a plate reader (PerkinElmer, USA). Absorbance readings are taken at regular time intervals ranging from 3 to 24 h. The percentage of hemolysis was calculated using the following formula.
%Hemolysis = 100 ×
Absorbance
of
sample - Absorbance
of
negative
control
[
Absorbance
of
Positive
control - Absorbance
of
negative
control
]

HeLa and L929 cells were added to DMEM medium containing 10% of FBS supplemented and 1% antibiotic-antimycotic solution. These cells were cultured at 37 °C in humidified CO2 incubator for 24h and observed the cells are forming a monolayer in the 96 well plate. The serum-free medium containing different (10–100 μg/ml) concentrations of CNDs were added to the cells and incubated again for 24 h. Discard the medium without disturbing the cell monolayer. Each treated cell were added with 100 μl of dimethyl sulfoxide and absorbance was taken at 570 nm to evaluate the cell viability. The cell viability was calculated using the following formula.
Cell viability (%) = Atreated/Acontrol X 100
where Atreated is absorbance of the treated cells with CNDs and Acontrol is the untreated cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing of dopamine based on conductivity
ce:section.ce:para: 3 ml of CNDs (1 mg/ml) were added to a fixed volume of 0.4 μl of FeSO4 (1 mg/ml) to form CND-Fe composites. The prepared composite solution was incubated for 15 min. To these composites, increasing concentration of DA from (400–1600 μg/ml) is added and the total volume was made up to 5 ml using HEPES buffer. The conductivity of the samples was measured using a EUTECH PCD 650 (Model no: 486679, Singapore).

The CNDs formation was visually confirmed by observing the emission of green photoluminescence (PL) observed in UV chamber operated at 365 nm. Furthermore, the absorption peak obtained at 280 nm from UV–Vis spectroscopy measurements confirmed the formation of CNDs from custard apple peel waste biomass (CAPWB) (Fig. 1 ). π-π* transition of the aromatic C–C bonds associated with CNDs contributed the absorption at 280 nm and n- π* transition of C–O bonds of carbon core attributed to absorption tail at 300 nm [35].

The major constituent of custard apple contains various compounds such as antioxidants, flavonoids, phenolic compounds, vitamins A, B, C, E, polyunsaturated fatty acids and also a variety of compounds like acetogenins [36]. There are twenty seven different active compounds are present in the custard apple peel [37]. All these compounds were undergone the hydrothermal carbonization and leading to the formation of CNDs. The CNDs are known to have various energy level functional groups due to which emissive traps will develop on the surface of CNDs. The surface of the CNDs is oxidized when they are excited at a certain wavelength which leads to the formation of surface defects. Due to these surface defects, CNDs absorption peaks will appears at higher wavelength [35]. Furthermore, it is observed that there is no significant reduction in the PL intensity of CNDs, therefore confirming their photo stability. The utilization of the custard apple peel waste for biomedical applications is illustrated in Scheme 1 .

CNDs shape appeared to be spherical as confirmed by TEM and HRTEM analyses ( Fig. 2 a and 2c). DLS measurement of CNDs depicts the size ranges from 3 to 8 nm which fulfill their unique characteristics (Fig. 2b). The bright circular rings corresponding to (0 0 2) and (1 0 1) planes indicates that the synthesized CNDs are crystalline in nature (Fig. 2d).

XRD spectrum of CNDs depicts peaks at 2θ = 21.1° and 37.5° corresponding to [0 0 2] and [1 0 1] planes, respectively (Fig. 3a) [38,39]. The poor crystalline nature of CNDs was confirmed by the interlayer d-orbital spacing of the carbon dots (d = 0.42 nm) which is greater than interlayer of graphite (d = 0.33 nm). FT-IR spectra of CNDs showed an intense peak at 3391 cm−1 corresponding to the hydroxyl functional group. This O–H can be used for the linking of DNA, RNA, protein, and cells (Fig. 3b) [21,40,41]. Various peaks corresponding to their functional groups observed at 2910 (C–H), 1651 (C–C) and 1420 cm−1 (alkenes C–O–C stretching vibrations). These peaks clearly demonstrates the CNDs have undergone partial oxidation. The oxidation is due to the irradiation of the solvent (H2O) and water present in CAPWB during hydrothermal preparation. Additionally, we observed O–H or C–O stretching vibration peaks at 1061 cm−1 [11,40,41].

3T3 Swiss fibro blast, C2C12, HeLa and E.coli were incubated with synthesized CNDs for 4 h and observed a bright photolumescence (PL) on the surface of membrane and inside the treated cells. The cells treated with CNDs exhibited bright PL emission near green (500–550 nm) and red (580–650 nm) when excitation of 450–490 nm and 548–580 nm, respectively (Fig. 6 ). This confirms that the synthesized CNDs exhibit PL phenomenon. These CNDs may use clathrin-caveolae-mediated endocytosis, for the internalization of nanoscale size thereby emitting PL [43].

P states of the sp2 sites in any carbon derived materials which has the mixture of sp2 and sp3 bonding determines the opto-electronic properties [44]. However, it is worthwhile to mention that the sp2 clusters p and p* electronic levels are always lie within the band gap of s and s* states of the sp3 matrix. These sp3 matrix are strongly localized on the surface of CNDs [45]. The PL in such carbon systems is a consequence of geminate recombination of localized e–h pairs in sp2 clusters. These clusters essentially behaves as the luminescence centers or chromophores [46]. The PL band gap depends on the size, shape, and fraction of the sp2 domains of carbon nanomaterials, tunable PL emission can be achieved by controlling the nature of sp2 sites. Thus, the synthesized CNDs exhibits the PL phenomenon in different cell types and also in bacteria. These results confirms the PL emitting capability of the synthesized CNDs at different excitation points and suggest their use as a bio imaging tool in various biomedical applications.

RBCs plays a key role in transporting gases, namely oxygen and carbon dioxide and occupy 40–50% (v/v) of human whole blood. Due to their easy availability, easy handling and abundance, these cells have been widely used for biomaterials evaluation for biological applications. Also, these cells give I n-vitro preliminary information about the interactions of biomaterials with other mammalian cell membranes. Specially, these cells are best suited for the evaluation of the blood contact-biomaterials. In this study, experiments were performed to evaluate the interaction of CNDs with RBC membrane. RBC was incubated with different concentration of the CNDs ranging from 250 to 750 μg/ml. It was observed that the cells morphology was not affected even at the highest concentration (750 μg/ml) in 3 h (Fig. 7 a). However, the morphology of RBCs crenated slightly at concentrations of 500 and 750 μg/ml after 24 h of the treatment. It is worthwhile to mention that there was no significant change observed at a concentration of 250 μg/ml after 24 h. This signifies that the synthesized CNDs can be used as a blood contact-biomaterial up to a concentration of 250 μg/ml.

The release of hemoglobin from RBCs is referred as hemolysis. It also reflects the degree of compatibility of CNDs with RBC membrane. RBCs were incubated with two different concentrations (250 and 500 μg/ml) of CNDs for 24 h and observed that there was insignificant lysis when treated with 250 μg/ml, whereas 50.7% lysis was observed in the RBCs treated with 500 μg/ml of CNDs (Fig. 7b). These results suggested that the synthesized CNDs below a concentration of 250 μg/ml are not influencing the morphology or integrity of the RBCs, thus can be used for blood contacting biomedical applications.

It is proposed that the RBCs interact with biomaterials by means of electrostatic or hydrophobic forces [47]. In this case, the electrostatic interaction of CNDs with RBCs are seems to be impossible since the former posses negative zeta potential. However, the hydrophobic forces may be possibly mediates the interactions between RBC and CNDs. It is reported that the hydrophobic aromatic rings forms the inner core of the CNDs during their synthesis by hydrothermal carbonization [42,48]. These hydrophobic aromatic rings mediate the interactions with the hydrophobic lipid bilayer of RBCs, thereby affecting their integrity.

MTT assay was carried out to determine the toxicity of the synthesized CNDs on HeLa and L929 cells. The assay was carried out by varying the concentrations of CNDs (10–100 μg/ml). It was observed that HeLa and L929 cells were showed 85% of viability even at the concentration of 100 μg/ml of CNDs after 24 h of incubation (Fig. 8 ).

However, few studies reported that quantum dots (QDs) showed apparent toxicity and low viability on different cells types [49]. On other hand, some reports also stated that CNDs are non toxic even at concentration as high as 500 μg/ml [50]. Though the reports are contrary to each other, the toxicity of the CNDs or quantum dots (QDs) are mainly based on the various factors including the type of method chosen for their synthesis, their shape and size. In this study, the CNDs were synthesized by merely irradiating the custard apple peel waste biomass (CAPWB) using microwave. There is no chemicals used or harsh methods followed. This may be the reason, unlike those toxic QDs, the synthesized CNDs showed good compatibility with the cells. Thus, these CNDs can be considered as nontoxic for typical cellular experiments, especially when compared to other nano dots for which cytotoxicity has been cited in the literature as a significant concern [51].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing of dopamine based on conductivity
ce:section.ce:para:
Conductivity-based detection methods are versatile techniques which can be used for the detection of many analytes of wide range including proteins, ions, nucleic acids and other biological analytes. In this connection, some elements must be incorporated into the nanofluids (CND solution in this case) that is sensitive to the presence of the analyte of interest. We have used Iron(II) as an element to demonstrate the detection of dopamine, a biological analyte, based on the conductivity phenomenon. We observed that the PL of CNDs was quenched when adding Fe(II) ions to the CNDs solution (Fig. 9 a). However, it was observed that the PL is tremendously increased by the addition of dopamine (DA) to the CND-Fe complex solution (Fig. 9a). We exploited this phenomenon for the detection of DA in combination with conductivity. The capacity of the materials to conduct electric current is termed as conductivity. The purpose of carrying out this experiment is to evaluate the conductivity of CNDs and to determine the effect of dopamine on this phenomenon. Various concentrations of DA were added to equal ratio of CND-Fe complexes (1 mg/ml of CNDs: 1 mg/100 ml of Fe), and it was observed that the conductivity increased with increased concentration of the DA (Fig. 9b). The conductivity of CNDs is originated due to the presence of sp2 C–C bonds on the surface of CNDs which was evident from the FT-IR studies. These C–C bonds aids in conjugation of the adjacent π-bonds to form the π-π*bands leads to the passage of current [52]. These results demonstrated a novel method to determine the concentration of DA, thus, can be used for electrical based sensing of DA. The benefits of using conductivity-based detection methods includes label free detection and are able to sense a wide range of analytes from ions to nucleic acids to proteins, and other biologically important bio analytes. The different methods of dopamine sensing are tabularized in Table 1
.

This study reports the use of biomass waste for the synthesis of CNDs which can be used for the biomedical applications. We have demonstrated the synthesis of carbon nano dots (CNDs) using custard apple peel waste biomass (CAPWB). These synthesized CNDs were spherical in shape and size ranges from 3 to 8 nm as evident from the transmission electron microscope analysis. The FT-IR analysis showed that the CNDs are associated with functional groups which may be originated from the biomolecules present in the custard apple peel. These functional groups may help in conjugation/fabrication with biomolecules such as proteins, carbohydrates and nucleic acids (DNA and RNA). Also, they showed a unique property namely wavelength dependent photoluminescence (PL). The synthesized CNDs can be used as a bioimaging tool as they showed the photoluminescence in normal, cancer and bacterial cells which was evident from the fluorescent studies upon treatment with synthesized CNDs. This property enables the synthesized CNDs in replacement of the synthetic dyes which are toxic and environmental un-friendly. Cytotoxicity of any materials is very important if they employ for the biomedical applications. The cells treated with various concentrations of CNDs showed different cytotoxicity, however, it is evident that 86% of cells were surviving even at 100 μg/ml of CNDs. It is concluded that the cytotoxicity analysis of the synthesized CNDs showed non toxicity which can be used for the biomedical applications. Additionally, these CNDs showed the excellent compatibility with blood thereby using them for biomedical applications. Importantly, we report the conductivity-based detection of DA where C–C bonds of the CNDs aids in conjugation of the adjacent π-bonds to form the π-π*bands lead to the passage of current thereby using them for sensing of DA. The method demonstrated here is chemical free, cost-effective and environmentally benign method to synthesize biocompatible, hemocompatible CNDs which can be used for the optical sensing and conductivity based detection of DA.

Punuri Jayasekhar Babu: Formal analysis, designed present research work, executed, involved in experimental work, analyzing data and preparation of manuscript. Saranya Sibyala: carried out some of the experiments. Yanghom Disco Singh: carried out some of the experiments. Mallepogu Venkataswamy: carried out some of the experiments. Ashok M. Raichur: Formal analysis, analyzing data and involved in the manuscript preparation, Writing – original draft. Mukesh Doble: Formal analysis, analyzing data and involved in the manuscript preparation, Writing – original draft.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: Authors are declared that there is no conflict of interest among us.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Dr. Punuri J Babu would like to acknowledge Science and Engineering Research Board (SERB) for providing the financial support vide project no: SRG/2020/002283 and also Central Instrumentation Facility (CIF), IIT Madras.

Renew. Sustain. Energy Rev.

Renew. Sustain. Energy Rev.

J. Food Sci. Technol.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon nanomaterials in agriculture: a critical review
Front. Plant Sci.
